questions.question:
Consider the following algorithm for searching for a given number x in an unsorted array A[1..n] having n distinct values:
1. Choose an i at random from 1..n
2. If A[i] = x, then Stop else Goto 1;
Assuming that x is present in A, what is the expected number of comparisons made by the algorithm before it terminates?
A. n
B. n-1
C. 2n
D. n/2
questions.to_find: Expected number of comparisons before the random search algorithm finds x

understand.plain: We pick a random index i from 1 to n each time and check if A[i] equals x. We repeat until we find x. Since picks are random WITH replacement (the same index can be picked again), the number of trials until the first success follows a geometric distribution. The probability of success on each trial is 1/n (only one element equals x), so the expected number of trials is n.

keywords.explain: Models the number of trials needed to get the first success, where each trial is independent with probability p of success. Expected value = 1/p.
keywords.example: Flipping a fair coin until heads: p=1/2, expected flips = 2.

keywords.explain: The average outcome over many repetitions of a random experiment.
Picking an index uniformly at random each time, independently, so the same position can be revisited.
keywords.example: In array [3,7,2,9,5], randomly picking index each trial until we find target.

understand.visual_alt: An array A of n elements; one element equals x. Each trial picks a random index uniformly from 1..n; the probability of hitting x is 1/n per trial.

given.aim: Find the expected number of comparisons until a randomly chosen index matches x.

terms.meaning: Size of the array A[1..n], all elements distinct.
terms.example: n=5 means the array has 5 elements.
terms.connects: Determines probability of success per trial: p = 1/n.

terms.meaning: Probability that a random index i satisfies A[i] = x on any single trial.
x definitely exists in A, so the algorithm will eventually terminate.
terms.example: Without this, it could loop forever.
terms.connects: Ensures the geometric distribution applies and E[trials] is finite.

given.plan: Model each trial as a Bernoulli experiment with success probability p = 1/n. By the geometric distribution, the expected number of trials to get the first success is 1/p = n. Each trial makes exactly one comparison, so expected comparisons = n.

steps.title: Model the probability of success per trial
steps.apply: There are n elements in A and exactly one equals x. Each index is chosen uniformly at random, so P(A[i] = x) = 1/n on each independent trial.
steps.note: The picks are with replacement (the algorithm can revisit the same index), which is why each trial is independent.

steps.title: Apply Geometric Distribution formula
steps.apply: The number of trials T until the first success follows Geometric(p=1/n). E[T] = 1/p = n. Each trial makes exactly one comparison, so expected comparisons = n.
steps.note: This matches option A. Note that options B (n-1), C (2n), D (n/2) correspond to other search strategies (linear search on average, etc.) but not this random-with-replacement approach.

solution.result: Expected number of comparisons = n. Answer: A. n

questions.formula_note: Geometric distribution: if each trial has success probability p independently, the expected number of trials until first success is 1/p. Here p = 1/n, so E[trials] = n.

questions.to_find: Which input causes the erroneous binary search program to fail (infinite loop or wrong answer)

understand.plain: The given binary search code has a bug: when the target x is not found and i < j, it sets i = k (instead of i = k+1) or j = k (instead of j = k-1). This means i and j can stop converging and get stuck in an infinite loop. We need to find which input triggers this infinite loop or incorrect behavior.

keywords.explain: Correct binary search must narrow the range [i,j] strictly each iteration: set i = k+1 when Y[k] < x, and j = k-1 when Y[k] > x.
keywords.example: Range [0,9]: if mid=4 and Y[4]<x, next range should be [5,9], not [4,9].

keywords.explain: If i = k and j = k do not strictly shrink the interval, then with i < j we may have i+1 = j, k = i, and the loop repeats without progress.
keywords.example: i=4, j=5: k=(4+5)/2=4; if Y[4]<x, set i=4 again. Loop repeats forever.

keywords.explain: An array where all elements are identical; the else branch (j=k) fires every iteration regardless, quickly making i==j.
keywords.example: Y=[2,2,2,2,2,2,2,2,2,2]: every comparison either matches x or goes to else.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='520' height='180' viewBox='0 0 520 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='500' height='50' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='260' y='42' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Y = [2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2],  x = 5 (> 2)</text><rect x='10' y='75' width='500' height='90' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='260' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>i=0, j=9 → k=4, Y[4]=2 < 5? No (2<5=true) → i=k=4</text><text x='260' y='122' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>i=4, j=9 → k=6, Y[6]=2 < 5? → i=k=6 ... i=8, j=9 → k=8</text><text x='260' y='144' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>i=8, j=9 → k=8, Y[8]=2 < 5 → i=8 (NO CHANGE!), loop stuck</text><text x='260' y='166' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>INFINITE LOOP: i=8, j=9, k=8 repeats forever</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Array of all 2s with x=5>2: i and j converge to i=8,j=9, then k=8 every iteration, Y[8]=2<5 so i stays 8, infinite loop.

given.aim: Identify which specific input causes the buggy binary search to fail (loop infinitely or give wrong answer).

terms.term: Bug: i = k instead of i = k+1
terms.meaning: When Y[k] < x the correct step is i = k+1, but the code sets i = k. This can freeze i in place.
terms.example: i=4, j=5, k=4: Y[4]<x → i=4 (unchanged), loop repeats identically.
terms.connects: Causes infinite loop when i+1=j and Y[i]<x.

terms.meaning: Loop continues while x not found AND search interval is non-empty. If i never reaches j, loop never ends.
terms.example: If i=8, j=9 forever, i<j stays true and Y[k]!=x stays true.
terms.connects: The infinite loop occurs when neither condition becomes false.

terms.term: All-equal array with x not present
terms.meaning: Y=[2,2,...,2] and x>2: every element is smaller than x, so the 'if' branch always fires (i=k), causing the interval to not shrink at the last step.
terms.example: When i=8, j=9: k=8, Y[8]=2<5, so i=8. Next iteration: same state. Infinite loop.
terms.connects: This is option C and is the triggering condition.

given.plan: Trace through each option to see which one causes i and j to stop converging. The bug is i=k (should be i=k+1), so whenever the search narrows to i+1==j and Y[i]<x, we get an infinite loop. Option C (all 2s, x>2) always fires the 'if' branch, leading to this scenario.

steps.title: Identify the bug and the triggering condition
steps.formula_raw: Correct: i = k+1; Buggy: i = k
steps.apply: The bug is in the line 'if(Y[k] < x) i = k'. Correct binary search should use i = k+1 to exclude k. With i=k, if we reach a state where i+1==j (adjacent indices), then k = (i+j)/2 = i (integer division). If Y[i] < x, then i=k=i — no change. The loop condition (i<j) stays true, Y[k]!=x stays true: infinite loop.
steps.note: This stuck state requires: (1) i+1==j, (2) Y[i] < x. We need an input that drives the search into this state.

steps.title: Test option C: Y=[2,2,2,2,2,2,2,2,2,2], x=5 (x>2)
steps.apply:
Start: i=0, j=9, k=4. Y[4]=2 < 5 (true) → i=4.
Now i=4, j=9, k=6. Y[6]=2 < 5 → i=6.
Now i=6, j=9, k=7. Y[7]=2 < 5 → i=7.
Now i=7, j=9, k=8. Y[8]=2 < 5 → i=8.
Now i=8, j=9, k=8. Y[8]=2 < 5 → i=8. (i unchanged!)
Loop condition: Y[8]!=5 (true) && 8<9 (true) → loop repeats forever. INFINITE LOOP.
steps.note: The other options (A, B, D) either terminate correctly or exit with the correct 'not in array' message.

solution.result: The program fails (enters an infinite loop) for option C: Y = [2 2 2 2 2 2 2 2 2 2] and x > 2.

questions.formula_note: Binary search correctness requires strict interval shrinkage: i = k+1 (not i = k) when Y[k] < x; otherwise adjacent-index states cause infinite loops.

questions.answer: A. Change line 6 to: if (Y[k] < x) i = k + 1; else j = k - 1;
questions.to_find: The correct fix to the erroneous binary search so it works for all valid inputs

understand.plain: The bug is that when Y[k] < x the code sets i = k (not i = k+1), and when Y[k] >= x the code sets j = k (not j = k-1). Both updates fail to exclude the already-checked midpoint k, causing the search interval to not shrink properly. The fix is to use i = k+1 and j = k-1 to strictly narrow the search range.

keywords.explain: After checking midpoint k, the next search should exclude k itself: if Y[k]<x, search right half starting at k+1; if Y[k]>x, search left half ending at k-1.
keywords.example: Range [0,9], k=4, Y[4]<x: new range is [5,9] (i=k+1=5).

keywords.explain: Using i=k instead of i=k+1 (or j=k instead of j=k-1) keeps the midpoint in the search range, possibly looping forever.
keywords.example: i=4,j=5: k=4, if i=k=4, nothing changes next iteration.

understand.visual_alt: Correct binary search: after comparing Y[k] with x, exclude k from next range by using i=k+1 (left is too small) or j=k-1 (right is too large). This guarantees the interval strictly shrinks each iteration.

given.aim: Fix the binary search so it terminates correctly for all sorted arrays and all values of x.

terms.meaning: The line 'if(Y[k] < x) i = k; else j = k;' uses k instead of k+1 and k-1.
terms.example: When Y[k] < x: i should become k+1 (exclude k from search), not k.
terms.connects: Fixing to k+1 and k-1 ensures strict interval shrinkage and termination.

terms.term: Option A: i = k+1; j = k-1
terms.meaning: The standard correct binary search update: if Y[k]<x the target is strictly right of k so i=k+1; if Y[k]>=x the target is at k or left so j=k (but here we use k-1 since midpoint != x was checked implicitly by the loop).
terms.connects: This is the classical correct binary search update, option A.

terms.meaning: Option B still uses i=k (same bug). Option C changes the loop termination condition to wrong logic (loop while equal). Option D uses i<=j which only changes termination, not the interval shrinkage bug.
terms.example: Option D with i<=j still loops forever on the all-2s array because i never passes j.
terms.connects: Only option A fixes the root cause.

given.plan: Analyze each proposed fix. The root cause is that i=k and j=k can leave the midpoint in the next search range, causing infinite loops. Only option A (i=k+1; j=k-1) eliminates k from the next range, guaranteeing the interval shrinks by at least 1 each iteration.

steps.title: Identify root cause
steps.formula_raw: Invariant: interval [i,j] must strictly shrink each iteration
steps.apply:
Current code: 'if(Y[k] < x) i = k; else j = k;'
When Y[k] < x: i is set to k. Next iteration's range is [k, j], which still includes k. If k is computed as the same midpoint again, no progress.
When Y[k] > x: j is set to k. Next range is [i, k], again including k. Same problem.
The fix must exclude k: use i = k+1 when Y[k] < x, and j = k-1 when Y[k] > x.
steps.note: Note that when we reach the else branch (Y[k] >= x), since we already know Y[k] != x (from the loop condition check would handle Y[k]==x exiting), we want j=k-1. But option A uses j=k-1 in the else, which covers Y[k]>x; if Y[k]==x the loop exits anyway.

steps.title: Verify option A fixes the infinite loop case
steps.formula_raw: if(Y[k] < x) i = k+1; else j = k-1;
steps.apply:
Re-trace with Y=[2,2,...,2], x=5:
i=8, j=9, k=8. Y[8]=2 < 5 → i = k+1 = 9.
Now i=9, j=9, k=9. Y[9]=2 < 5 → i=10.
Loop check: i < j? 10 < 9? FALSE. Loop exits.
Y[k] = Y[9] = 2 != 5 → prints 'x is not in the array'. Correct!
Also, if x is present, Y[k]==x exits the loop correctly.
steps.note: Option A is the standard textbook binary search update; it guarantees O(log n) termination.

solution.result: The correction is option A: Change line 6 to 'if (Y[k] < x) i = k + 1; else j = k - 1;'

questions.formula_note: Correct binary search update rule: i = k+1 when Y[k] < x (target is right of k); j = k-1 when Y[k] > x (target is left of k). This ensures the search interval shrinks by at least 1 per iteration, guaranteeing O(log n) termination.
